name: Dr. Robert Bullard
title: Distinguished Professor of Urban Planning and Environmental Policy
relevance: Known as the 'Father of Environmental Justice.' Has spent decades documenting the disproportionate siting of polluting facilities in communities of color. Our cross-linked TRI-health-demographics database is the kind of tool his research has called for since the 1990s. A collaboration or endorsement would carry enormous weight.
notes: Author of 'Dumping in Dixie' and 18+ books on environmental justice. Founded the Bullard Center for Environmental and Climate Justice at TSU. Would likely value a free, open dataset linking TRI releases to community health outcomes at national scale.

name: Dr. Sacoby Wilson
title: Associate Professor, Maryland Institute for Applied Environmental Health
organization: University of Maryland School of Public Health
relevance: Leads research on environmental justice, cumulative environmental health impacts, and community-engaged research. Our dataset directly supports his work on how pollution burden maps onto community demographics and health outcomes. Has published extensively on the need for better cross-linked environmental health data.
notes: Directs the Center for Community Engagement, Environmental Justice, and Health (CEEJH). Active on social media and frequently engages with data-driven environmental justice tools. Strong potential for collaboration on analysis using our dataset.

name: Dr. Paul Mohai
title: Professor, School for Environment and Sustainability
relevance: Co-author of foundational environmental justice research demonstrating racial and socioeconomic disparities in proximity to hazardous waste and polluting facilities. Our database provides the kind of national-scale, facility-level cross-linked data his research methodology requires.
notes: Published influential studies on environmental inequality using EPA data. Co-founded the Michigan Environmental Justice Coalition. Would likely be interested in using our cross-linked dataset for updated national analyses.

name: Dr. Rachel Morello-Frosch
title: Professor, Department of Environmental Science, Policy, and Management and School of Public Health
relevance: Leading researcher on environmental health disparities, cumulative impacts, and the intersection of pollution exposure with community vulnerability. Has published on the need for integrated databases combining pollution source data with health and demographic indicators — exactly what our project delivers.
notes: Co-PI on CalEnviroScreen, which is a state-level model for what we are building nationally. May be interested in how our national cross-linked dataset compares to or extends California-specific tools.

name: Earthjustice (Environmental Law Program)
title: Managing Attorney or Senior Data Analyst
relevance: The largest nonprofit environmental law organization in the US. Litigates cases on behalf of communities harmed by toxic pollution. Our facility-level database linking chemical releases to community health outcomes could directly support their litigation research, helping identify the most impacted communities and quantify harms.
notes: Has offices across the country. Frequently files lawsuits involving TRI-reporting facilities. A searchable database of releases cross-linked to health outcomes would be a valuable litigation research tool.

name: Environmental Defense Fund (Health Program)
title: Director of Health or Senior Scientist
relevance: One of the largest environmental advocacy organizations globally, with significant focus on chemical safety and health. EDF has advocated for stronger TRI reporting requirements. Our dataset adds the community health and demographic context that raw TRI data lacks, which supports EDF's advocacy for chemical policy reform.
notes: Has a dedicated chemicals and health team. Publishes analyses of toxic chemical exposure. Would benefit from a tool that connects TRI facility releases to county-level health outcomes at national scale.

name: NAACP Environmental and Climate Justice Program
title: Director of Environmental and Climate Justice
relevance: The NAACP's environmental justice program works at the intersection of civil rights and environmental health. Our database enables communities and advocates to quickly identify which TRI facilities are operating in their counties and what health outcomes look like in those areas. Directly supports their 'Just Energy' and environmental justice campaigns.
notes: Published the 2012 report 'Coal Blooded' on coal plant impacts in communities of color. Our tool provides updated, broader data covering all TRI-reporting industries, not just power plants.

name: WE ACT for Environmental Justice
title: Executive Director or Director of Policy
relevance: Community-based environmental justice organization focused on Northern Manhattan and national policy. Advocates for communities disproportionately burdened by pollution. Our free, searchable database would empower their community organizing by making facility-level pollution and health data accessible without requiring technical expertise.
notes: Based in Harlem, NYC. Focused on empowering communities with data and advocacy tools. Our dashboard's county-level search and filtering is designed with exactly this kind of community use in mind.

name: ProPublica (Environment Reporting Team)
title: Reporter or Editor, Environment Desk
relevance: ProPublica has published major investigations into toxic pollution and environmental justice, including their 'Sacrifice Zones' series mapping cancer-causing industrial air pollution. Our cross-linked dataset of 22,936 TRI facilities with health outcomes across all 50 states offers new story angles on which communities bear the highest pollution burden alongside the worst health outcomes.
notes: Already built similar tools (Sacrifice Zones cancer risk map). Would likely be interested in a complementary dataset that adds county-level health outcomes and demographic context to facility-level release data. Data is available in journalist-friendly CSV and Excel formats.

name: Inside Climate News
title: Reporter or Editor
relevance: Pulitzer Prize-winning nonprofit news organization covering climate and environment. Has published investigations into pollution impacts on communities. Our national database of toxic releases cross-linked to health outcomes provides a data-driven foundation for investigative stories on which industries and chemicals are most harmful to community health.
notes: Strong focus on accountability journalism. Our dataset enables reporters to identify patterns — such as which states have the highest carcinogen releases, or which industries release the most toxics in counties with the worst health outcomes — that could anchor investigative series.

name: The Guardian (US Environment Desk)
title: Environment Reporter or Editor
relevance: The Guardian's US environment desk regularly publishes data-driven stories on pollution, environmental justice, and corporate accountability. Our dataset of 155,000+ chemical release records across 22,936 facilities, linked to community health data, offers compelling story angles on environmental disparities at the county and state level.
notes: Has published major features on Cancer Alley in Louisiana, Flint water crisis, and other environmental justice stories. Our data covers all 50 states and could support both local deep-dives and national analyses.

name: Bloomberg Philanthropies (Environment Program)
title: Program Director or Senior Associate, Environment
relevance: Bloomberg Philanthropies funds major environmental initiatives including the Beyond Carbon campaign and supports data-driven approaches to environmental policy. Our structured database fills a gap in publicly available environmental health data that could support Bloomberg-funded initiatives focused on reducing pollution in vulnerable communities.
notes: Bloomberg has invested heavily in data infrastructure for policy change (Bloomberg Government, Bloomberg NEF). Our project aligns with their approach of using structured data to drive environmental outcomes. Potential for funding or data licensing.

name: Grist
title: Data Reporter or Editor
relevance: Grist is an independent nonprofit media organization focused on climate and environmental justice. They regularly produce data-driven features and interactive tools. Our dataset of toxic releases cross-linked to community health outcomes directly supports the kind of environmental justice reporting Grist specializes in.
notes: Published interactive features on environmental justice, including coverage of EPA EJScreen and cumulative impacts. Our tool adds a dimension EJScreen lacks: direct linkage of facility-level toxic releases to county health outcomes.

name: Sierra Club (Environmental Justice and Community Partnerships)
title: Director of Environmental Justice or Senior Campaign Representative
relevance: One of the largest grassroots environmental organizations in the US with chapters in every state. Their environmental justice campaigns focus on reducing pollution in overburdened communities. Our searchable database enables local chapters to quickly identify TRI facilities, release volumes, and health outcomes in their communities.
notes: Has local chapters that could use our tool for community-level advocacy. Their Beyond Dirty Fuels and Clean Transportation campaigns could benefit from data on industrial releases by state and county.

name: Natural Resources Defense Council (Health and Environment Program)
title: Senior Scientist or Director, Health and Environment
relevance: NRDC combines science, law, and policy expertise to protect public health and the environment. Their health and environment program works on toxic chemical exposure and has published reports using TRI data. Our cross-linked dataset adds the health outcome and demographic context that makes TRI data actionable for policy advocacy.
notes: Has published reports on toxic chemicals, air pollution, and water contamination using EPA data. Our database provides the cross-linkage to health outcomes that their analysts currently have to build manually for each report.

name: Center for American Progress (Energy and Environment Policy Team)
title: Senior Fellow or Director, Energy and Environment
relevance: CAP is a leading policy institute that produces research and policy recommendations on environmental justice. Their energy and environment team has published analyses on pollution burden in disadvantaged communities. Our national-scale database would support their policy research with ready-made cross-linked data.
notes: Frequently publishes policy briefs and reports that cite EPA TRI data. Our dataset eliminates the manual effort of joining TRI data with health and demographic data, which is currently a bottleneck for policy researchers.

name: RTI International (Environmental Sciences Division)
title: Director or Senior Research Scientist, Environmental Sciences
relevance: RTI is a major nonprofit research institute that provides environmental data services to government agencies including EPA. They conduct environmental health assessments and build data tools for regulatory agencies. Our cross-linked database methodology and dataset could be licensed or used as a component in RTI's environmental health analytics offerings.
notes: Has active EPA contracts for environmental data analysis. Could be interested in licensing the cross-linked dataset or the methodology for building integrated environmental-health databases. Headquartered in Research Triangle Park, NC.

name: ICF (Environment and Planning Division)
title: Vice President or Director, Environment
relevance: ICF is a major government consulting firm with extensive EPA contracts, including work on TRI program support and environmental justice assessments. Our cross-linked database demonstrates the kind of integrated environmental-health data product that supports the work ICF does for federal and state agencies.
notes: One of EPA's largest contractors. Works on environmental compliance, risk assessment, and environmental justice screening. Could be interested in the cross-linked dataset as a data product or in hiring someone who can build these tools.

name: Abt Associates (Environment and Health Division)
title: Principal Associate or Division Director, Environment and Health
relevance: Abt Associates conducts environmental health research and consulting for EPA and other federal agencies. They specialize in quantifying the health impacts of environmental exposures. Our database linking TRI releases to county health outcomes is directly relevant to the kind of environmental health impact assessments Abt performs.
notes: Has conducted major studies on the health costs of pollution for EPA. Our dataset provides a ready-made national baseline for the kind of exposure-outcome analyses they perform. Potential for data licensing or collaboration.

name: Scorecard / GoodGuide (Historical Reference)
relevance: Scorecard.org (by Environmental Defense Fund) was a pioneering online tool that mapped pollution data by zip code. GoodGuide rated products on health and environmental criteria. Both demonstrated market demand for accessible pollution data. Our project updates and extends this concept by cross-linking TRI data with actual health outcomes and demographics — something neither predecessor achieved at this scale.
notes: Scorecard.org is now defunct. GoodGuide was acquired by UL (Underwriters Laboratories). The gap these tools once filled remains, and our project addresses it with modern cross-linked data. UL's Environmental Health and Safety division may be interested.

name: Dr. Lara Cushing
title: Assistant Professor, Department of Environmental Health Sciences
organization: UCLA Fielding School of Public Health
relevance: Researches cumulative environmental health impacts and environmental justice screening tools. Co-developed CalEnviroScreen, California's environmental justice screening methodology. Our national-scale cross-linked database extends the CalEnviroScreen concept to all 50 states, which would be directly relevant to her research agenda.
notes: CalEnviroScreen is the most successful state-level model for what we are building nationally. Dr. Cushing's expertise in developing these tools makes her an ideal research collaborator for validating and extending our methodology.

name: Dr. Ihab Mikati
title: Environmental Health Researcher
organization: EPA Office of Research and Development / University of North Carolina
relevance: Lead author of influential 2018 study in the American Journal of Public Health showing that people of color are disproportionately exposed to air pollution from industrial facilities. Our database provides the facility-level cross-linked data needed to update and extend this type of analysis nationally.
notes: Published 'Disparities in Distribution of Particulate Matter Emission Sources by Race and Poverty Status' which is foundational to current environmental justice quantification. Our dataset could enable updated analyses.

name: Union of Concerned Scientists (Climate and Energy Program)
title: Senior Analyst or Director, Climate and Energy
relevance: UCS publishes research on pollution, environmental justice, and corporate accountability using scientific data. Their analyses frequently draw on EPA data to quantify pollution impacts. Our cross-linked database provides a ready-made analytical tool that would support their research and advocacy on toxic chemical exposure and environmental justice.
notes: Published reports on disproportionate pollution burden in communities of color. Active in advocating for stronger chemical reporting requirements. Would value a free tool that makes cross-linked TRI data accessible.

name: Dr. Manuel Pastor
title: Distinguished Professor of Sociology and American Studies and Ethnicity
relevance: Director of USC's Equity Research Institute. Pioneer in using data to quantify environmental justice disparities. Has published extensively on the intersection of race, poverty, and pollution exposure. Our national cross-linked database is exactly the kind of data infrastructure his research group uses and advocates for.
notes: Co-author of research on cumulative environmental impacts and environmental justice screening methods. Active in California environmental justice policy. Would likely be interested in a national-scale tool.
